data.title: CEF Weekly Review: Are We In Another CEF Deleveraging Wave?
data.description:
Welcome to another installment of our CEF Market Weekly Review, where we discuss closed-end fund ("CEF") market activity from both the bottom-up – highlighting individual fund news and events – as well as the top-down – providing an overview of the broader market. We also try to provide some historical context as well as the relevant themes that look to be driving markets or that investors ought to be mindful of.

This update covers the period through the second week of April. Be sure to check out our other weekly updates covering the business development company ("BDC") as well as the preferreds/baby bond markets for perspectives across the broader income space.

Market Action

CEF sector returns were mixed on the week with Muni sectors underperforming and equity-linked sectors finishing in the green - the opposite of what we saw the previous week. The vast majority of sectors saw a drop in NAVs, primarily due to the rise in Treasury yields while all but one sector discount tightened.

Year-to-date, sector performance is mixed. Munis are still hanging on to mild outperformance while equity-linked sectors are deeper in the red.

April is shaping up to be the worst month for CEFs since late 2022.

The whipsaws in the CEF market are the largest in the post-COVID environment.

Discounts have recovered somewhat from the recent plunge but remain on the wider side of the recent range. However, what's interesting is that the market has generally weathered the situation relatively well. Discounts are not nearly as wide as they were in 2023 and the size of the sell-off has, so far, been relatively modest.

Market Themes

There was a question on the service about CEF deleveraging and whether that’s affecting a lot of funds at present. The short answer is no. If we take the highest volatility CEF sector - CLO Equity - NAVs here have fallen at most around 10-15% since the start of March (ECC showed a 10% drop in March with other sector CEFs at lower levels).

If we start off with a relatively high leverage level of 40%, the sell-off, if evaluated conservatively, only gets us to a bit below 45%. If we assume roughly half of the liabilities in debt and half in preferreds that gets us to a bond asset coverage of 3.9x (well above the 3x level) and preferreds asset coverage of 2.275x (more than 10% above the 2x level).

The NAV moves in other sectors are much more benign - the loan CEF sector NAV drop is around 5% which isn’t really going to make much of a difference on leverage. Realistically, we would need about the same kind of move in spreads from early March levels to the April peak for deleveraging to be a serious threat to CEFs.

As discussed a number of times, it’s generally best to avoid funds with strict CEF leverage caps (e.g. Nuveen and John Hancock preferreds CEFs, among others) as these funds may act preemptively even before the caps are hit. This would cause them to sell assets and only buy them back at higher prices, locking in losses for investors in a typically mean-reverting credit market.

Market Commentary

CLO Equity CEF NAVs are starting to come in. EIC had a 5% drop in March while ECC had a 10% drop with other sector funds in the middle of this range. That was on the back of a 0.65% widening in HY corporate bond spreads in March. Spreads are wider by around 0.8% so far in April, which could drive a similar drop in the sector if spreads remain stable. This means that the sector is not particularly cheap on a discount basis (with most funds likely trading at small premiums based on today's NAV) but decently valued on an underlying basis. When you have this discrepancy, discounts matter a bit less for allocation purposes, as the potential upward move in NAVs alongside higher yields are more important for total returns than discounts.

RiverNorth Capital and Income Fund (RSF) completed its quarterly repurchase offer. The fund has been buying back shares for a long while with a relatively generous offer of 5% share repurchase at the NAV. The latest pro-ration factor came in at 11% which is pretty good. The discount has been trading around 6-8% which offers a 2-2.5% annualized alpha on top of the historic performance. The fund is a nice diversifier to the typical CEF with a mostly small business whole loan portfolio. Ever since it redeemed its preferred, it has been trading with no leverage, making it a relatively low beta asset.

Stance and Takeaways

This week, we took advantage of both sharply wider credit spreads and discounts to top up our CEF allocation. Recall that we significantly took down our CEF exposure over the last 18 months on the back of expensive credit valuations, tight discounts and a low level of margin yield on leveraged assets. We added two CEFs - the Ares Dynamic Credit Allocation Fund (ARDC) and the PIMCO High Income Fund (PHK). The two are both multi-sector credit funds with strong performance and an attractive valuation.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: McDonald's: A Great Company To Hold Long Term, But Currently Too Overvalued
data.description:
Today, I want to take a quick look at McDonald's (NYSE:MCD) and explain why I rate the company as a HOLD. In my eyes, MCD is doing a great job growing their net income and FCF; the issue is that through the past years they have become overvalued. To understand why my rating is as such, we will take a look at the most recent earnings report, to then analyze the company's fundamentals through the 9 Pillar Analysis and finally calculate their intrinsic value using 4 of our intrinsic valuation models.

Analysis

Q4'24 Earnings Report

On the 10th of February, MCD announced their Q4'24 results. Their Revenue fell short of the $6.45B consensus mark, reported at $6.39B. Regarding the Non-GAAP EPS, that was also a miss by $0.03, reported at $2.83 but expected at $2.86.

Although these misses do not seem that bad, something that is important to understand is that the company's US-based sales have seen the worst drop since the pandemic, dropping by 1.40% compared to the consensus mark of 0.41%. Even though the company's global sales grew by 0.40%, better than the expected decrease of 0.93%, as the US represents 41.01% of the company's overall revenue, the revenue for Q4 was lower than in Q3 as already mentioned.

Normally, as I am a long-term investor, this small quarterly decrease wouldn't bother me, but in this case, I sense something bigger. As inflation has been really high through the past few years, which I already mentioned in numerous of my other articles, consumers have been heavily cutting on their spending. This is affecting all B2C businesses as they see an overall fall in sales volumes. Most of all, this is affecting non-essential products and services such as restaurants, cafés, home renovation and product stores, etc. One of such companies is MCD, which, as customers cut on spending, registers a decline in sales.

Before moving to the 9 Pillar analysis, there is one more thing I want to mention, and that is that through the past few years, MCD has been increasing their prices, which has been one of the reasons for their growing profitability and so net income. To put this into perspective, since 2019, average store pricing has increased by around 40.00%. Of course, the franchise can't raise their prices forever, so the question remains: what will happen when they can't increase the price tags on their products anymore?

In my eyes, these are all issues that can be solved and certainly don't put the company at high risk. What I am trying to say is that they might, in the future, see lower growth than what is expected, especially with rising inflation and possible tariffs, which would force consumers to spend even less on unnecessary eating out. Therefore, the company's current valuation is, in my eyes, too high compared both to their current and future earnings potential.

9 Pillar Analysis

To understand what I mean, we will take a look at the 5Y PE and P/FCF, Pillars 1 and 2, but first, let me explain what the 9 pillars actually are.

Pillars 1 and 2 represent the 5-year Price-to-Earnings (P/E) and Price-to-Free Cash Flow (P/FCF) ratios, respectively. Pillars 3, 4, and 5 illustrate the growth in revenue, net income, and free cash flow over the past 5 years. Pillar 6 focuses on the 5-year trailing profitability, while Pillar 7 highlights the change in shares outstanding. Pillar 8 tracks dividend growth, and Pillar 9 calculates the ratio of long-term liabilities to 5-year free cash flow minus dividends, which assesses a company's capability to pay down debt within a reasonable period while still paying dividends. MCD gets a rating of 6/9, with the three pillars in the red being the valuation and debt levels.

Valuation (Pillars 1 and 2)

Looking at the 5Y PE ratio, we can see that it is higher than the average stock PE of around 22.50, sitting at 24.94. Personally, I wouldn't mind paying 25x earnings for the company, as they are a well-established brand that has steady financial and dividend CAGRs. The issue is that currently, the PE is sitting at 27.14, which in my eyes is too expensive even for a well-established company like MCD.

Above you can see that indeed, today, MCD is trading the most expensive since 2020, even though the future growth prospects aren't too great.

Regarding the P/FCF, that is currently sitting at 33.45, higher than the 5Y average of 27.29, and the highest it has been over the past 5 years. So by looking at these two values, we can see that indeed, MCD is currently overvalued compared to what they have been trading in the past.

Financial Growth (Pillars 3, 4 and 5)

The reason why I don't rate the company as a SELL is because of their great financial growth through the past 5 years, which if continues, to an extent supports the company's higher valuation.

The revenue has been growing at a 5Y CAGR of 6.47%, from $19.21B in 2020 to $25.92B in 2024.

Though this isn't as impressive as the net income, which has been growing at a CAGR of 15.11% over the past 5Y, from $4.73B in 2020, to $8.22B in 2024. My sentiment is further supported by the 10Y CAGR, which is sitting at 6.30% with the net income growing very steadily from $4.53B in 2015 to the current $8.22B.

This is the same case with the FCF, which has grown from $4.62B in 2020, to $6.67B in 2024, accounting for a CAGR of 11.01%. The 10Y CAGR is also sitting at a decent value of 4.10%. So overall, the growth is very decent for a stable company like MCD, but with inflation growing and customers becoming more wary of their spendings, the current valuation is in my eyes too bullish.

Debt (Pillar 9)

Now, before moving to the dividends, let's take a look at the third pillar in the red, and that is the debt. The company's LTL / 5Y FCF - Dividends ratio is sitting rather high at 6.71, much higher than the below 3.00 we would like to see. Essentially, this means that MCD would take around 34 years to pay off all their LTL while paying out their current dividend. Over the past 5 years, their LTL have actually increased by 1.44%, from $53.57B to $54.34B, which isn't great as it reaffirms the view that the company is heavily indebted and isn't doing much to change it.

Furthermore, their Current Ratio is sitting at 0.94, meaning that they have more current liabilities than current assets, again, exposing the company to danger from financial downturns.

Dividends (Pillar 8)

Looking at the dividends, what I want to point out is that the company's 5Y CAGR is sitting nice and high at 7.72%, with a decent dividend yield of 2.29%. The FCF payout ratio is also sitting at a decent range of 68.22%, leaving MCD with enough FCF to push into further dividend increases, paying down debt, buying back shares, etc. Though with their high debt, I would expect their CAGR to decrease into the future.

Intrinsic Value

Nevertheless, now that we have gone through the 9 Pillars, let's calculate the company's intrinsic value through our models.

For the Graham's Formula, we will plug in the 5Y EPS of $10.46, and based on the historical 5Y and 10Y net income CAGRs, predict the forward EPS CAGR at 9.33%. This will give us an intrinsic value of $199.24, 35.65% lower than the current trading price of $309.61.

For the second model, we will as always look to the DCF model, where I will plug in the 5Y average FCF of $6.23B, and based off of that, calculate the future FCF using a CAGR of 7.00%, based on the historical 5Y and 10Y CAGRs and the terminal rate of 2.50%. Finally, I will discount the FFCF at a rate of 7.33%, my desired rate of return, and get an intrinsic value of $173.34.

For the third valuation, we will compare the MCD's PE to the PE ratios of Starbucks and Restaurant Brands International to get an intrinsic value of $263.24.

And Finally, using the alternative DDM, I will project a dividend CAGR of 6.67%, slightly lower than the historical 5Y YOY growth as I do believe that the company will face some pressure from their LTL levels. Then, using the stock price next year predicted by analysts, I will calculate an intrinsic value of $356.01.

After averaging out the four values, we will arrive to an ultimate intrinsic value of $247.96, 19.61% lower than the current trading price of $308.44. With a margin of safety sitting at 10.00%, our acceptable buying price would be $222.93.

Conclusion

Concluding the analysis, based on McDonald's steady financial and dividend growth, along with a well-established customer base, I do have to rate them as a HOLD, even though they do have quite high debt and are overvalued compared to their historical multiples, and future growth. I trust that the company will grow into the future, but not at the rate that is currently priced in; therefore, again, I have to rate them as a HOLD.

data.title: VTI: A Fund That Carries More Risk In The Current Economic And Political Environment
data.description:
Investing strategies sometimes have to change, even as long-term objectives remain the same. While overreacting to short-term market cycles is usually not productive, most people still have to be willing to change their approach as different sectors perform better in constantly evolving economic and market conditions.

The most successful strategy for investing over most of the last decade has been to focus on the indexes. The S&P 500 (SPY) and Nasdaq have both offered investors impressive gains during this time period, and allocating capital to these benchmarks has been a simple and effective strategy.

One of the more well-known exchange-traded funds that seeks to similarly track the overall market is the Vanguard Total Stock Market ETF (NYSEARCA:VTI). VTI tracks the CRSP US Total Market Index. The fund's inception date was in May 2021 and this ETF reconstitutes quarterly.

VTI has offered investors total returns of 180.2 percent over the last decade, while the S&P 500 (SPY) has offered investors total returns of 197.3 percent during this same time period. While both VTI and the S&P 500 seek to track the broader market, the key difference between them is that the Vanguard fund includes smaller-cap equities.

Today, I am reiterating my sell rating for the Vanguard Total Stock Market ETF. This fund has more risk because of the investment's heavy exposure to big-cap tech and the current trade war between the US and China, and VTI's holdings in the technology sector are also not diversified. There are also signs the current economic slowdown is accelerating.

VTI has an expense ratio of .03 percent, 1.70 trillion in assets under management, and a current yield of 1.45 percent. The ETF's holdings are 31.60 percent technology, 14.01 percent the financials, 10.97 percent health care, 10.28 percent consumer cyclicals, 9.40 percent communication, 7.43 percent industrials, 6.28 percent consumer defensives, 3.23 percent energy, 2.80 percent utilities, 2.24 percent real estate, and 1.76 percent basic materials. VTI's largest holdings are Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), NVIDIA (NVDA), and Amazon (AMZN). These 4 positions make up nearly 23 percent of the overall fund's assets.

The main reason VTI has performed well over the last 2 decades is because of the fund's significant big-cap tech holdings, but these assets carry more risk now. The tech industry gets nearly 60 percent of this sector's revenue from outside of the US, and most of the companies VTI has as core holdings, such as Apple, are heavily dependent on China, a country the US is currently in an increasingly hostile trade war.

This Vanguard fund has nearly 32 percent of the ETF's assets in the technology sector and several of this investment's core holdings, including Apple and Amazon, rely heavily on the world's second-largest economy for revenue. While Trump has temporarily exempted smartphones and computers from the tariffs he imposed on China, the Chinese government is still retaliating against US companies and these exceptions may not be permanent.

This Vanguard fund's lack of diversity makes the investment much more susceptible to the underperformance of the core holdings, which make up between 4-6 percent of the overall fund. VTI also has significant exposure to NVIDIA, which makes up nearly 6 percent of the overall fund, and this company has struggled over the last year as the chipmaker's growth rate has moderated with the company now maturing and also facing new competition in the cloud space and from startups.

There are also multiple indicators that the overall growth rate in the US and abroad is slowing. Recent retail spending numbers have come in soft, new auto sales were down significantly in February, and consumer debt remains at record levels. China also continues to deal with a housing crisis that is impacting consumer debt levels as well. While many large-cap tech companies should continue to perform well, VTI has significant exposure to a narrow group of companies that are heavily leveraged to the world's second-biggest economy.

All investment theses have risks, and Trump and President Xi of China could reach a resolution to these nations' trade concerns anytime soon. The Federal Reserve has also been hawkish on rates, but if Powell were to relent and begin to lower rates like his European counterparts, there would likely be a rally in equities and the dollar would likely fall against most major foreign currencies. VTI has significant international exposure since this fund primarily invests in large-cap companies.

VTI has performed reasonably well over the last decade, but this Vanguard's heavily concentrated positions in a narrow group of large-cap tech holdings should make returns more volatile moving forward, particularly in the current less predictable political and economic environment. While index investing has worked very well for some time, there are more diverse funds that still give investors exposure to big-cap tech and cyclical sectors such as the Capital Group Dividend Value ETF (CGDV) that should offer individuals a better risk profile moving forward.

data.title: NIE: This Equity/Convertible CEF Provides Tech Exposure With Reduced Downside Risk
data.description:
(Data below is sourced from the Virtus fund website unless otherwise stated.)

The Virtus Equity & Convertible Income Fund (NYSE:NIE) is a $660 million fund that invests at least 80% of the portfolio assets in a combination of equities and income producing convertible securities.

The equity portion varies from 40%- 80% of assets.

The convertible portion varies from 20%-60% of assets.

As of March 31, 2025, the fund had 57.5% invested in equities and 35.7% invested in convertibles with the rest in cash and a small amount of derivatives. No leverage is used.

The fund typically uses a covered call option strategy on about 70% of the stock positions. This allows the fund to earn additional income from selling the call option premiums and may help to reduce risk.

I last wrote about NIE about a year ago. The fund performed quite well throughout 2024 but has corrected significantly early in 2025 largely because of its large allocation to technology and the Mag 7 stocks.

Discount History

The fund is currently trading at a –10.01% discount to NAV which is in line with its 5 year average discount of -10.20%. But over the last year, the average discount was about –8.4%.

Here is the discount history from cefconnect:

Here is the NIE portfolio asset allocation as of March 31, 2025:

These allocations are dynamic and can change over time. But the managers generally keep the equity allocation very close to 60%.

Here is the sector allocation as of March 31, 2025. Information Technology and Financials were the highest two allocations, with close to a zero allocation to Energy stocks.
