Top Ten Holdings (as of March 31, 2025)

The fund is well diversified with high allocations to the largest mega cap companies.

Note that the fund has a 1% holding in the WFC-L preferred stock. Technically, it is a convertible preferred, but it is so far out of the money that it is really a “busted” convertible, that is more like a perpetual fixed income holding with long duration.

Fund Leverage Profile

NIE does not use any leverage.

Correlation Data

I looked at three month correlation data to see how the fund has been trading recently. Here are sample 3-month correlations for NIE compared to various peers or benchmark ETFs. The correlations are scaled between -100% to +100%:

NIE versus (SPY) = + 92% S&P 500 ETF

NIE versus (XYLD) = + 84% Global X S&P 500 Covered Call ETF

NIE versus (JEPI) = + 76% JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF

NIE versus (QQQ) = + 91% Invesco QQQ Trust

NIE versus (NVDA) = + 64% Nvidia common stock

(Source: portfoliovisualizer.com)

Note that the correlation of NIE with SPY and QQQ is quite high, largely because of its high info tech equity exposure.

Fund Credit Rating Distribution (as of 03/31/2025)

Nearly all of the NIE credit holdings are unrated or BBB (at the low end of investment grade) using S&P's rating scale. This is typical of convertible offerings where the rated bonds usually fall in the lower end of investment grade at BB or BBB.

Many issues that trade in the convertible market are unrated. Some companies that issue convertibles have no other debt in their capital structure and the company may want to save money by not paying to get rated unless they know they will receive an investment grade.

But the future default losses of many of these issues could be fairly low if there are no higher rated bonds above them in the capital structure that would take priority in a distressed situation.

Quarterly Distributions

NIE has been paying out a steady $0.50 quarterly distribution for over three years. The quarterly distribution was raised from $0.38 to $0.50 at the end of 2021. They also paid out a large $1.754 special distribution at the end of 2021 and an even larger $1.9765 special distribution at the end of 2022.

In recent quarters, most of the distributions have been capital gains, but that varies with market conditions.

Basic Information

(NIE) Virtus Equity & Convertible Inc. Fund

  • Total Investment Exposure= $636 MM

  • Total Net Assets= $636 MM

  • Fund Type: Perpetual

  • Annual Distribution Rate= 9.68%

  • Dividend Frequency= Quarterly

  • Current Quarterly Distribution= $0.50 per share ($2.00 annually)

  • Baseline expense ratio= 1.07%

  • Portfolio turnover rate= 72%

  • Discount to NAV= -10.01%

  • 3 Year Avg. Discount= -10.27%

  • 5 Year Avg. Discount= -10.19%

  • Effective Leverage= None

  • Average 3 Mos. Daily Trading Volume= 71,000 shares

  • Average 3 Mos. Daily $ Volume= $1,500,000

Sources: cefconnect, Yahoo Finance

Summary and Trading Suggestions

Now that the major equity market indices and tech stocks have corrected significantly from their highs, it may be a good time to gradually tiptoe back into the equity markets using a fund like NIE.

NIE generally has reduced risk compared to SPY or QQQ, because of its allocation to convertibles and its hedging strategy of selling covered calls. Between the generous distribution yield over 9% along with additional "special" distributions in some years, you also receive some discount alpha by recovering some of the discount to NAV.

NIE normally trades with about a 10% discount. I would place it on a watch list for now or perhaps buy a small starter position of 100 shares for tracking purposes and then try to add more shares when the discount is above 10%.

NIE trades around 70,000 shares a day and has decent trading liquidity for smaller orders. The bid-asked spread is usually around five cents, or about 25 basis points. But it can vary throughout the trading day, and the spread can sometimes go to ten cents or higher.

I would recommend using limit orders when trading NIE if you use a zero-commission broker. For larger orders, divide up the order into smaller pieces and spread out your purchases. You may also want to gradually dollar cost average into NIE over several days.

data.title: VSDA: A Hidden Gem For Dividend Growth Alpha
data.description:
Last year, I did a deep dive on VictoryShares Dividend Accelerator ETF (NASDAQ:VSDA) and concluded the fund was a compelling dividend growth ETF worthy of consideration in one's portfolio, but ultimately gave it a Hold rating based on a few aspects about the funds screening methodology. Reading back, I think I was a little narrow in drawing my conclusion. At the time, I was seeking a suitable partner for my Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) position which I determined, after several rounds of research, that iShares Core Dividend Growth ETF (DGRO) was the best choice.

I still stand by my choice, but since growing both positions, I'm on the hunt to add more alpha into my dividend growth portfolio. I aim to enhance my dividend growth portfolio’s alpha and diversification by adding funds like VSDA, which may offer higher rates of dividend growth and exposure to early-stage dividend growth stocks not captured by my existing holdings.

Performance Comparison

Dividend Metrics

I checked in on VSDA recently and was impressed it's kept up uninterrupted double-digit dividend growth, posting a staggering 31.32% annual payout growth in 2024. So, I re-ran my Large-cap focused dividend growth ETF screener and found that of the 11 five year double-digit dividend growth ETFs, only VSDA and SCHD qualified as also having uninterrupted dividend growth since inception. I believe this is a hidden gem in the space as it only has $225M AUM compared to the $65B and $28.5B AUM of SCHD and DGRO respectively.

The funds yield has steadily risen since 2021, rising from 1.90% to 2.75%, making it even more enticing to add into a dividend focused portfolio.

Dividend Metrics VSDA SCHD DGRO
Dividend Yield ('TTM') 2.75% 4.09% 2.36%
5-Year Growth Rate 23.08% 11.44% 7.62%
CAGR to 2024* 17.11% 11.47% 8.85%
Years of Uninterrupted Growth 7 13 10
Yield on Cost (YOC) Since Inception 5.22% 12.25% 5.62%

*Starting after 1 year is complete to smooth the data.

I re-compiled the data from last year's analysis I did on VSDA, SCHD, and DGRO and added some additional dividend metric data. I love what I'm seeing:

  • A 5-year dividend growth rate of 23% with a 17% CAGR to back it up.
  • It's yield on cost is hot on DGRO's heels as well at 5.22% which is impressive being the youngest of the three funds.
  • At this rate, the funds YOC will be 10%+ in 4–5 years which is about how long it took for SCHD to achieve the same level.

Total Returns and Diversification

The total return of all three funds has been competitive as well, all posting a total return in the 120-130% range more or less since mid 2017 of VSDAs inception. It's interesting to see how each fund responds to the recent correction, which VSDA tends to be a little more volatile likely because it has less holdings. All in all, these funds more or less trend the same with no clear breakaway winner since VSDAs inception.

Last, let's take a look at the diversification side of things. For reference, SCHD and DGRO overlap 18% by weight. VSDA overlaps SCHD and DGRO 20% and 26% respectively. It's interesting that VSDA and SCHD only have 12 overlapping holdings when each fund is only about 76 and 100 holdings respectively and have strict screening criteria. In general, I think there is value add and not too much overlap especially for SCHD holders. Looking at DGRO it is a bit redundant, and can be seen as a refined screening on DGRO to add more concentration into top growth holdings.

Dividend Growth Alpha Thesis

VSDA looks at 14 independent variables to try and correlate the likelihood of having a positive change in annual dividend growth, which puts into action how we can find alpha in my strategy different from SCHD or DGRO

The VictoryShares Dividend Accelerator ETF offers exposure to large-cap U.S. stocks, that feature not only a history of increasing dividends, but which also possess the highest probability of future dividend growth. It seeks to provide exposure to dividend growth, rather than yielding, offering a potential diversification benefit to high dividend yielding alternatives, particularly in a rising rate environment.

I really like the funds focus on all things dividend growth, especially the ability to invest in companies early in their dividend growth lifecycle. For example, several of the Mag 7 have recently initiated a dividend policy, but funds like SCHD and DGRO will not even screen them until they have been paying or growing for 5+ years. VSDA only holds MSFT currently as does DGRO, but it's possible we could see more companies like Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL), Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), and perhaps NVIDIA Corp (NVDA) in the future.

Looking at the top 10 holdings, much of the funds dividend growth isn't derived from massive payout growth of the underlying holdings, which are all under 10% growth. The bottom 65 holdings average low-mid teens from my estimate as well. What significantly drives the growth of dividend growth funds, like VSDA, is the rebalancing (swapping of stocks growing faster) and the capital appreciation of the funds AUM which buys more of these dividend growers, contributing to higher absolute dividend dollars being paid out.

Example

Consider a portfolio of three stocks A, B, C paying $1.70 in dividends

  1. Dividend Growth of the underlying
  • Stock A grows their dividend 10% from $1 to $1.10
  • Stock B grows their dividend 5% from $0.50 to $0.525
  • Stock C grows their dividend 5% from $0.20 to $0.21

The new payout is $1.835 or 7.94% dividend growth.

2. Rebalancing/Reconstituting the fund

Replace stock C with a new Stock D which grew 50% from $0.20 to $0.30. The new payout is $1.925 or 13.24% dividend growth.

3. AUM Growth

Assets grow 15% so all the dividend dollars scale up accordingly

  • Stock A grows their dividend from $1.10 to $1.265
  • Stock B grows their dividend from $0.525 to $0.60
  • Stock D grows their dividend from $0.30 to $0.345

The new payout is $2.21 or 30.22% dividend growth.

Outlook

The risk to my thesis is if VSDA posts a negative payout growth or starts really underperforming SCHD and DGRO in terms of total return and a lot of capital gets tied up in the trade. I think the first point is more likely as aggressive dividend growth is harder to maintain long-term as much as I want to be a part of it. How I'm handling this is carving out a portion of my portfolio to take a smaller position in VSDA and also looking into Amplify CWP Enhanced Dividend Income ETF (DIVO) and AAM Brentview Dividend Growth ETF (BDIV) as additional funds to generate dividend growth alpha in my portfolio. This would create a "fourth" position as my "dividend growth alpha" different from core holdings like SCHD, DGRO, and my international Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF (SCHY).

data.title: Heineken: Buyback And Emerging Markets Upside
data.description:
The earnings season kicked off positively with Heineken (OTCQX:HEINY), which delivered strong results ahead of Wall Street expectations. We previously reported our preference for Fundamentals Over Short-Term Miss, and considering the recent market turmoil, the company's YTD stock price has been up by 14.85%. Aside from a long-standing buy rating on Heineken's EverGreen strategic plan, 2) beer premiumization evolution, 3) discount peers' valuation, and 4) higher market share penetration. In Q4, we also analyzed the following downside risks: lower U.S. discretionary spending and unsupportive EM trends; however, the company's guidance was unchanged.

Before the Q1 earnings season, we also flag adverse calendar effects, but we prefer Brewers over Spirits. Given the negative share price performance, we are currently overweight Diageo and Davide Campari. Brewers' companies have a limited tariff impact and a higher exposure to European consumers than to US consumers. Spirit players are also more negatively impacted by a weak dollar, which, conversely, is a transactional FX tailwind for brewer companies.

Q1 Results and Our Upside

Last time, we considered Q1 non-supportive mainly due to calendar effects, but Heineken reported a beer volume decrease of 2.1% with a net revenue acceleration of 0.9% (Fig 1). Aside from the top-line disclosure, the company did not report any update on the P&L. Cross-checking analysts' consensus, Heineken once again beat expectations, and Q1 volumes are growing, excluding technical headwinds from later Easter timing, one less selling day, and phasing headwinds. The company also reported weak results on EU pricing negotiations.

Source: Heineken Q1 2025 Results ppt - fig 1

Why are we still positive?

  1. (2025 Outlook confirmed despite tariff uncertainty). The company reiterated its Fiscal Year 2025 core operating profit growth of 4-8%, factoring in (Fig 2): 1) volume growth by +1%, 2) at least €400 million gross cost savings target, and 3) limited US tariff impact. In detail, the company's operations in the US focus primarily on importing and distributing its portfolio of beers and cider. Tariff development is still to be seen, but the company disclosed that "over 95% of our volume is locally produced, our brewery footprint is advantageous." Additionally, the company might be impacted by higher aluminum costs, which might increase packaging costs for canned beverages;
  2. (Supportive Evergreen strategy). Heineken Evergreen's strategy is working well, supported by high-quality volume MIX (the premium beer segment is outperforming with a Q1 growth of +1.8%), and it is gaining market share in more than 50% of countries;
  3. (Emerging Markets Upside). Several EMs are recovering following external shocks over the past 2 years. Compared to our last update, on the upside, emerging market volume momentum is picking up. The company positively reported Vietnam, India, and Ethiopia as "promising volume growth, benefitting from the strategic actions taken." During the Q&A analyst call, we learned that Heineken continues to increase marketing expenses. The company reported a positive sell-out in Brazil, with accelerated share gains. In Vietnam, the company is up by +14%, while in India, the company should benefit from the recent price increase in its largest state, Telangana, and the opening of Andhra Pradesh sales. Nigeria and Ethiopia are back to volume growth, supported by pricing action that offset FX devaluations. Compared to last year, the beer category in Mexico showed a slight decline due to the impact of Easter timing. Key to report is the fact that beer premiumization is working, and if there is a pick-up in EM volume growth, we see Heineken's EBIT margin as achievable in 2025;
  4. (The buyback is still to be executed). The company has a €1.5 billion buyback firepower. Including 11 April 2025, Heineken repurchased a total of approximately €87 million worth of shares. Therefore, we have current downside protection.

Fig 2

Earnings Changes and Valuation

In our last assessment, we already set lower expectations for the Q1 results; having said that, we believe the company is rebuilding investor credibility. With better-than-estimated results, we are slightly increasing the company's 2025 financials. We confirmed sales estimates at €30.75 billion, but we are more supportive of margins. This is backed by beer premiumization and volume recovery in EM. We forecasted an EBIT growth of 6.5%, while today, we believe there is an expectation to predict a higher core operating profit, which is also aligned with the company's guidance. CAPEX was unchanged, so our 2025 core operating profit moved from €4.8 billion to €5 billion. This implied an EBIT increase of 7.5%. We previously anticipated a negative FX of €80 million in our estimates. With the current FX translation, we now guide €180 million headwind on net profit, which implies almost a 4% impact on EPS. We reached €2.95 billion in net profits, with an EPS of €5.15.

Despite a positive YTD price performance, the company trades on a 15.3x P/E basis (without considering its buyback). Additionally, the company pays a dividend of €1.86 per share. Therefore, a total yield (with the buyback) of >5.5% is combined with a volume recovery strategy, efficiency gain, and a structural profit growth story. Thus, Heineken's valuation remains attractive. On a valuation basis, it trades below 1) its five-year historical average (21.45x - Fig 3). With a higher EPS and an unchanged P/E target of 20x, we increased our buy rating with a target price of €103 ($58.5 in ADR).

Fig 3

Risks

We believe Heineken management is more prepared for macro risk and has agility in investment decisions. In our estimates, we consider a weak US environment and negative EPS revisions from recent FX swings, particularly considering a stronger Euro. We also report our previous downside risks: 1) potential higher corporate tax, 2) advertising restrictions, 3) unfavorable weather conditions, 4) lower cost savings, and 5) clients' preference for non-alcoholic products (even if the company is moving with its zero-alcohol beer strategy).

Conclusion

Despite challenging market momentum, the company is executing and confirming its 2025 outlook. We positively report the buyback upside, and with the EM volume growth pick-up. For this reason, we confirm our buy rating estimates.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Low Growth, High Returns: The Jabil Paradox
data.description:
Investment thesis

Operating in a mature sector, Jabil Inc. (NYSE:JBL) has steadily improved its returns through operational efficiency despite delivering a low revenue growth rate. While EPS lagged peers before 2020, Jabil now leads on EPS and return on capital. The company has demonstrated improved asset utilization and a reasonable Reinvestment rate.

However, its increasingly leveraged balance sheet and reliance on short-term liquidity tools to fund share buybacks raise concerns about the sustainability of capital return policies. At the same time, Jabil’s intrinsic value is below the current market price based on conservative assumptions. This is not an investment opportunity from a value investment perspective.

Business background

Jabil is a global provider of manufacturing services and supply chain solutions. Its core business is contract manufacturing with a strong focus on electronics. Jabil segments its operations into two major reporting segments (prior to Sep 2024):

  • Electronics Manufacturing Services (EMS) – High-volume, low-mix products with cost-efficient, automated manufacturing.
  • Diversified Manufacturing Services (DMS) – High-complexity, low-volume products requiring deeper customer collaboration and specialized expertise.

In 2015, the DMS segment was the largest revenue contributor, accounting for about 60% of the revenue. However, the EMS segment had a bigger revenue growth rate, so that by 2024, the DMS segment only accounted for about 48% of the revenue. Refer to the right part of Chart 1.

While it maintains strong international operations, Jabil has progressively diversified away from Asia, placing greater emphasis on the Americas and other global regions. For example,

  • In 2015, China, Singapore, and Malaysia accounted for about 57% of the total revenue.
  • In 2024, these 3 countries accounted for about 32% of the total revenue

This shift probably aligns its footprint with evolving client needs and geopolitical considerations. Refer to the left part of Chart 1.

Jabil operates in the electronics manufacturing services industry. The industry is a mature market, characterized by single-digit growth rates driven by outsourcing trends, increasing product complexity, and demand across diverse end-markets such as automotive, healthcare, and consumer electronics.

  • “The global electronic manufacturing services market size was valued at USD 626.8 billion in 2024 and is estimated to grow at 5.1% CAGR from 2025 to 2034.” Global Market Insights
  • “The global electronic manufacturing services market size is calculated at USD 617.90 billion in 2025…accelerating at a CAGR of 6.95% from 2025 to 2034.” Precedence Research

Operating trends

Given the market growth picture, you should not be surprised to find that Jabil’s revenue only grew at 4.4% CAGR from 2015 to 2025.

Note that Jabil has Aug as its financial year-end. Unless stated otherwise, the year in this article refers to the financial year. The 2025 values in this article are based on the LTM Feb 2025 performance.

You can see from Chart 1 that the revenue in 2024 was lower than that in 2023 by about USD 5.8 billion.

  • USD 2.4 billion came from the strategic exit from the mobility and legacy networking businesses.
  • The balance of USD 3.4 billion is attributable to soft demand across multiple end-markets.

This suggests that the revenue contraction in 2024 was not solely strategic - it also reflected transitory weakness in end-market demand. This is further evidenced by the flat quarterly revenue over the past five quarters, indicating a pause in growth momentum.

Notes to Chart 2:

a. SGA margin = Selling, Administration, and General expenses/Revenue

b. I broke down the operating profits into fixed costs and variable costs.

  • Fixed cost = SGA, Depreciation & Amortization, R&D, and Others.
  • Variable cost = Cost of Sales – Depreciation & Amortization.
  • Contribution = Revenue – Variable Cost.
  • Contribution margin = Contribution/Revenue.

On a positive note, gross profit margins and Selling, General, and Administration (SGA) margins show signs of improvements, as seen on the left side of Chart 2. This trend is also visible in the contribution margin, illustrated on the right side of the chart.

Importantly, Jabil’s cost structure is resilient, with fixed costs accounting for only about 8% of total costs. This low fixed-cost base helps shield profits from revenue fluctuations.

Over the past decade, Jabil’s returns, while volatile, have shown an uptrend. Refer to the left part of Chart 3.

The returns were also high averaging 16.8% ROIC and 24.4% ROE over the past 11 years. These exceeded their respective cost of funds, indicating that shareholders' value was created.

My analysis showed that the main driver seemed to be better capital turnover and not just better margins.

As shown in the right part of Chart 3. Jabil’s effective tax rate has been volatile over the past decade. It ranged from a low of 20.2% in 2024 to a high of 76.6% in 2018, with an average of approximately 40.8%. The volatility is the result of:

  • The one-off impact of U.S. tax reform.
  • A complex global footprint with shifting income distributions.
  • Changes in valuation assumptions around deferred tax assets and intercompany charges.

In recent years, Jabil's effective tax rate has normalized closer to 20–30%, suggesting greater stability going forward.

The picture that emerges is that, while Jabil operates in a low-growth, single-digit sector, it has sustained profitability through an efficient cost structure. Although there have been positive trends in gross profit and SGA margins, the more significant contributor to profitability has been improved asset utilization.

Peer comparison

Jabil competes with a mix of global EMS providers and diversified manufacturers. As such, I compared its performance with a sample of companies operating in these sectors, as shown in Table 1.

Jabil is among the bigger companies based on the 2024 revenue and ranked No. 3 in terms of the revenue growth rate. You can see that, except for Berry Global Group, all the other companies delivered single-digit revenue growth rates.

Compared to its peers, Jabil may not have always led on margins or cash flow. But it has outperformed its peers where it counts - on capital efficiency and long-term earnings growth. The transition from lagging EPS to industry-best post-2021, along with an improving free cash flow margin, underscores a well-executed operation. Refer to Charts 4 and 5.

  • Jabil had the best return on capital over the past decade.
  • It had an average EBIT margin.
  • Like the peers, Jabil’s levered free cash flow margin was volatile compared to the other 3 metrics. While having among the worst margins most of the time, this improved to rank No. 2 in 2024.
  • Jabil’s EPS was among the worst from 2015 to 2020. But it became the best post-2021.

In a mature and competitive sector, Jabil’s ability to sustain returns, navigate volatility, and improve shareholder metrics reflects strong operational leadership and a resilient business model.

Financial Position

At first glance, Jabil’s financials look concerning:

As of the end of February 2025 had a very high Debt Equity ratio of 243%. This is the highest over the past 11 years.

Next, from 2015 to 2025, it had a Reinvestment rate (defined as Reinvestment/NOPAT) that averaged 45%.

Reinvestment = CAPEX & Acquisitions – Depreciation & Amortization + increase in Net Working Capital.

I consider this a high rate when compared to one given by the fundamental growth equation of growth = Return X Reinvestment rate. Based on the 4.4% revenue growth rate and an average ROIC of 16.8% over the past 11 years, the fundamental Reinvestment rate = 26%.

Thirdly, there are some concerns about the capital allocation plan. Refer to Table 2. While the cash flow from operations was more than sufficient to fund its CAPEX, it was not enough to fund its share buyback. There was a large increase in receivables sold to financial institutions - a standard working capital and liquidity management tool – in 2017 and 2018 to help fund this.

But there were some positive points:

  • As of the end of Feb 2025, it had USD 1.6 billion in cash, equal to about 9% of the total assets.
  • From 2015 to 2025, there were only 2 years when it failed to deliver a positive cash flow from operations. It also had a good cash conversion ratio, as during this period, it generated USD 10.3 billion in cash flow from operations compared to the cumulative PAT of USD 5.5 billion.

My findings suggest that Jabil’s core operations are financially sound - it converts earnings into cash reliably, maintains strong liquidity, and reinvests with discipline.

However, its capital structure has become increasingly leveraged, and some shareholder returns have been funded through short-term liquidity tools rather than retained earnings or organic free cash flow.

This raises questions about balance sheet risk and the sustainability of capital return policies, particularly in the context of a downturn or a tighter credit environment. The aggressive share buyback program, while improving capital efficiency, is a key contributor to this concern.

That said, my assessment is based on an 11-year cumulative view. If Jabil can align its buybacks with annual cash flow and manage short-term liquidity effectively, the financial picture may not be as concerning as it first appears.

You can understand why I would rate Jabil as moderately sound financially.

Valuation

I adopted the following picture in valuing Jabil.

  • The base revenue would be the 2025 revenue with a 4% perpetual growth rate.
  • The base contribution margin would be the past 11 years' average margin. This would improve proportionately by 10% in the terminal year.
  • The base Reinvestment rate would be the past 11 years' average rate. This would be reduced to one given by the fundamental growth equation in the terminal year.
  • The tax rate would be based on the 2023 to 2025 average tax rate.
  • There would not be further share buybacks. As such, an improvement in profitability is attributable to the operations and not due to a reduction in capital.

Based on these assumptions, I obtained an intrinsic value of USD 115 per share compared to the market price of USD 134 per share (as of 16 April 2025). You can see that there is no margin of safety.

Valuation model

I valued Jabil based on a multi-stage Free Cash Flow to the Firm (FCFF) model, as shown in Table 3.

The basic equations used in the model are:

FCFF = EBIT(1 – t) – Reinvestment.

EBIT = Revenue X Contribution margin – Fixed cost

The contribution margin and fixed cost are based on the operating model shown in the right part of Chart 2.

The Reinvestment was derived from the Reinvestment margin.

Notes to Table 3:

a. No change

b. Pegged to the growth rate

c. Assumed proportionate improvement

d. Assumed growth at terminal rate

e. Revenue X Margin, and after accounting for Fixed costs

f. Assumed proportionate improvement

g. b X f

h. FCFF for each year = e - g

j. Refer to the WACC table

k. NPV for each year = (h X j)

l. Terminal for the year discounted at a terminal growth rate

m. 5 years NPV + terminal value

n. Inclusive of any excess TCE. Non-operating assets, MI, and Debt

o. Based on the number of shares

The WACC used in the valuation model was based on the first page results of a Google search for “Jabil WACC”. Refer to Table 4.

Risks and limitations

Jabil operates in a mature sector. The key driver of profitability is improving efficiency. In my valuation model, this is reflected in improving the contribution margin and the Reinvestment rate.

But I think my assumptions are a bit conservative looking at Chart 6.

  • The projected FCFF is less than that based on a simple log-normal trend line projection shown in the dotted line in the right part of Chart 6.
  • The terminal revenue is about the same as the peak revenue achieved by the company in 2023. Refer to the left part of Chart 2.

Furthermore, the terminal ROIC of 21% is even lower than the 2025 ROIC of 26%.

Given the conservative assumptions, there is probably some upside. But this has to come from improving efficiency. For example, if all the other parameters in the valuation model remain unchanged:

  • The intrinsic value increases to USD 149 per share if the terminal contribution margin is 20% higher instead of 10%
  • The intrinsic value increases to USD 122 per share if the capital turnover is based on the past 2 years' average instead of the past 11 years' average.

I have not adopted these better rates as I think there are limits to efficiency improvements. For example, from 2015/16 to 2024/25, gross profit margin only improved by 9%. As such, while conservative, I think my assumptions are based on the track record.

Conclusion

Jabil operates in a mature industry with modest structural growth, yet it has carved out a resilient business model. Over the past decade, it has demonstrated solid operational execution and improving returns.

Its cost structure and asset utilization have enabled it to sustain profitability over the past 11 years. But the growing financial leverage and reliance on short-term liquidity tools to support shareholder returns warrant closer attention. The aggressive share buyback program has enhanced capital efficiency but also introduced balance sheet risk that may become more pronounced in a downturn or a tighter credit environment.

Nonetheless, Jabil’s ability to consistently convert profits into cash, with a reasonable Reinvestment strategy suggests that it is well-positioned operationally. If capital returns can be better aligned with annual free cash flow, the financial picture becomes more sustainable.

Based on my analysis, Jabil stands out as a moderately sound business with a strong operational core. However, my valuation did not provide any margin of safety. As such, I do not consider this an investment opportunity from a value investment perspective.

data.title: Salesforce's Bet On Agentic AI Is Just Getting Started
data.description:
Introduction:

Salesforce (NYSE:CRM) is one of the most dominant and innovative software companies on the planet, offering a suite of enterprise tools centered around customer relationship management with a recent focus on agentic AI. Over the past year, Salesforce has demonstrated its ability to grow their top line while simultaneously improving margins across the board. This firm appears to be entering a new phase of growth powered by AI which positions Salesforce as a key player in the future of enterprise automation. With solid financial results, a growing number of customers utilizing AI features, and multiple strategic partnerships with Fortune 500 companies, Salesforce is building a platform that should serve as the backbone of many of the largest companies on the planet going forward.

Financials:

Salesforce reported full year FY25 revenue of $37.9 billion, a 9% increase YoY, with a non-GAAP operating margin of 33% (250bps improvement YoY). Net income rose nearly 50% YoY to a staggering $6.2 billion, driven by expanding gross margins (77.2% vs 75.5% last year) and slower growth in operating expenses (3.5% growth YoY compared to 8.7% YoY revenue growth).

This demonstrates the immense operating leverage of their business model, as the marginal cost of adding new customers is very low and fixed expenses tend to rise at a slower rate than new customer acquisitions.

The company ended the year with ~$60 billion in remaining performance obligations, and is currently guiding sbetween $40.5-$40.9 billion in FY26 revenue (7%-8% growth) and a 100bps improvement in non-GAAP operating margin to 34%.

This exceptionally strong financial performance highlights Salesforce's ability to scale their operations and generate massive profits. With increasing adoption of consumption-based pricing models and digital labor within their own operations, the business is positioned well to sustain high single digit to low double digits revenue growth rates and margin expansion over the coming years.

Catalysts:

Salesforce's AI powered Agentforce platform is seeing very strong early adoption, with over 3,000 customers already onboarded. Salesforce itself is already using Agentforce to drive internal efficiencies as they have handled over 380,000 support requests using AI agents with an 84% autonomous resolution rate.

In the earnings call, the company also highlighted how these tools have been very effective in increasing their selling capacity as their sales productivity has jumped by 7% since implementing these solutions and AI agents are delivering over 50 personalized outbound leads on a daily basis. These results demonstrate a quantifiable ROI of implementing AI agents, which should be a key selling point as more enterprises look to adopt AI to create efficiencies and reduce expenses.

As large customers ramp up their usage of the Agentforce platform, consumption-based pricing tied to agent activity will become a powerful and large new revenue for Salesforce, which should help them sustain top line growth into the future.

Salesforce's internal usage of Agentforce has led to other major efficiencies, such as a 30% productivity gain in engineering, which has led them to freezing hiring of new engineers. Salesforce wants to become the leading provider of digital labor across the globe, not by building out massive data center infrastructure themselves but by using cloud providers infrastructure to power intelligent software solutions.

The Data Cloud now contains over 50 trillion individual records of structured data such as customer profiles, support tickets, invoices, or product listings. This vast dataset is essential for training AI agents and providing businesses with actionable insights into their customer behavior patterns.

With 25% of this data now coming from external sources (thanks to their Zero Copy architecture) companies can now add Salesforce directly into their tech stack without the need to manually transfer data. This evolution positions Salesforce as not only a CRM provider, but as a central data platform for enterprises. As companies continue to consolidate their data into Salesforce's system, it will drive greater platform usage, higher retention, and increased cross-selling opportunities across their broader product suite.

Agentforce's growing partner network is another significant catalyst for future revenue growth, as it enhances the platform's flexibility and appeal across a wide range of enterprise environments. Through partnerships with some of the largest cloud service providers such as Google and AWS, customers can now deploy pre-built AI agents with minimal setup which should increase adoption for certain use cases.

The recent expansion of Salesforce's partnership with Google allows companies to integrate the Gemini LLM directly within Agentforce and run Salesforce applications on Google Cloud. This not only improves data privacy but also increases the platform's relevance to enterprise IT initiatives. This compatibility should make Agentforce very appealing for large companies already using AWS or Google Cloud, as it removes any friction from adopting agentic AI solutions and should broaden Salesforce's total addressable market considerably.

Additionally, by supporting multi cloud deployments Salesforce should be able to win over large enterprises with complex cloud environments who want to avoid vendor lock-in (if a particular cloud provider were to raise their prices, companies can easily switch cloud providers and continue using Agentforce).

As more organizations begin to adopt AI tools that integrate seamlessly into their existing cloud ecosystems, Salesforce stands to benefit from larger deal sizes and higher consumption-based revenues.

Finally, the upcoming launch of Agentforce 2dx and AgentExchange represents another growth avenue for Salesforce, as it will unlock a new set of monetization opportunities. Agentforce 2dx is the latest evolution of Salesforce's flagship AI offering that is designed to operate behind the scenes without constant human oversight and intervention.

This platform will allow businesses to automate complex workflows across various business segments and operations such as customer service, sales, finance, accounting, and more. With API integration, companies can now embed Agentforce into backend systems (such as automatically notifying customers of delays triggered by ERP systems) which will create real-time AI agent interactions.

At the same time, Salesforce is introducing new tools to help enterprises easily configure and deploy AI agents at scale, which will slowly lower barriers to adoption and expand usage across organizations.

Complementing this is the introduction of AgentExchange which is a marketplace for pre-trained, industry-specific AI agents. Developers can list and monetize specialized agents tailored to various verticals such as finance, healthcare, retail, and more, and enterprises can purchase and deploy these agents easily.

This model is spectacular as it not only will create a new developer ecosystem, but it will also create a new recurring revenue opportunity for Salesforce by monetizing access to high value add, ready to use agents. Together, the Agentforce 2dx and AgentExchange platforms help position Salesforce at the epicenter of the growing enterprise Ai economy.

Risks:

Salesforce currently faces several risks that could impact its growth trajectory and long-term performance metrics. The current strategic shift towards a consumption-based pricing model, particularly for AI-driven products like Agentforce and Data Cloud, may lead to higher long-term revenue per customer, but at the present this model introduces uncertainty in revenue recognition and contract predictability.

Unlike the traditional seat-based model, which generates an incredibly predictable recurring revenue stream based on the number of employees at a firm, the consumption model is entirely tied to how many customers are using the platform (the volume of data processed or the number of AI agent tasks executed during a period).

Usage patterns may fluctuate wildly based on customer deployment speed or economic conditions, which makes it difficult for Salesforce to accurately predict contract value or future revenue growth rates with precision. Salesforce currently suggests that customers begin by implementing agentic AI solutions slowly with routine tasks, which are unlikely to generate substantial revenue. However, as enterprises realize the effectiveness of this technology, they are likely to increase their usage, and in turn the amount of money they will pay Salesforce.

Finally, as Salesforce continues to expand into new markets and innovative use cases, they must manage the operational difficulties of helping customers monitor and control their usage of AI agents. If customers struggle with budgeting or optimizing consumption, it could lead to churn or pushback on pricing, which is a clear headwind.

All in all, while this model supports scalability and aligns with industry trends, specifically cloud service providers business models, it also adds a layer of volatility and execution risk as Salesforce and their clients adapt to this evolving landscape.

Valuation:

To determine Salesforce's intrinsic value, I created a discounted cash flow model. The perpetual growth rate I used in the model was 2.5% and my projection covers the next five years. Additionally, the figures for cash, debt, and shares outstanding were taken from the most recent earnings report.

For my cost of capital, I first computed the cost of debt using the most recent interest expense, debt and tax rate information available. Next, I calculated the cost of equity using the yield on a 10-year U.S. treasury bond as my risk-free rate, the beta from S&P Capital IQ, and the equity risk premium from professor Aswath Damodaran's website. Below are the calculations for both WACC and implied share price:

My revenue growth rates were in line with consensus estimates, and for the EBIT figures I settled on expanding margins by 50bps each year due to solid operating leverage present in their most recent results. Based on these assumptions, I arrived at an equity value of ~$271 billion and an implied share price of $281.67. This represents a solid upside of 12.7% based on the current price of $249.84 as of April 16th at the market close. I believe that my base case for margin expansion is relatively conservative, especially if Salesforce is able to continue replacing employees with digital labor.

Conclusion:

Salesforce's transformation into a comprehensive enterprise intelligence platform has positioned this firm to appreciate substantially based on projected future financial results. The growing adoption of Agentforce and strategic alignment with hyperscalers like Google and AWS are strong tailwinds that should lead to continued growth in the top and bottom line.

While the current shift to consumption-based pricing introduces some uncertainty, it also reflects a more scalable business model that should pay dividends in the future. With solid fundamentals and very visible catalysts, I am rating shares of Salesforce a BUY at current levels.

data.title: Q2 2025 Equity Market Outlook
data.description:
By Raheel Siddiqui

While surging tariffs and a hard sell-off have sown uncertainty, we expect negotiations to bring some relief on initial tariff proposals, and that sharply slower growth seems more likely than a U.S. recession. We also believe stimulus in Europe and China may rejuvenate global industrial activity (albeit at a slower pace), and recommend styles, sectors and regions that are most geared to it.

Investment Themes and Views1

We have retooled the methodology behind our equity recommendations to provide more actionable guidance for active asset allocators and portfolio managers. Our methodology is designed to assess developing risk and opportunity cycles at the index and subcomponent levels (countries, sectors, regions and styles) and be more responsive to changes in market sentiment. The targeted investment horizon for these recommendations is approximately 12 months, but we expect more frequent adjustments at the sub-index level.

Tarrifying Shocks

The announcement of DeepSeek’s cost-efficient generative AI model in January rattled the Magnificent 7,1 the main drivers of the broader market’s bull run in 2023 and 2024. Already under pressure from DOGE cuts, weakening CEO and consumer sentiment, the rapid escalation of the trade war hit the U.S. equity market hard: As of the close on April 4, the S&P 500 Index had tumbled more than 1000 points (17.4%) from its peak, generating widespread recessionary fears.

While we believe an overdose of growth-inhibiting policy can push even a relatively healthy economy into recession, we think the U.S. hasn’t yet reached that point, and that the market’s action thus far is incongruent with the onset of a recession. Rather, we think the performance during the sell-off suggests relative strength in some of the more cyclical economic sectors in the U.S. and around the world:

  • Value stocks, which tend to be more sensitive to movements in the overall economy, outpaced growth stocks during the sell-off in both large caps and small caps.2
  • Traditionally cyclical sectors such as Financials, Industrials, Materials and Energy have outperformed the broader index.3
  • As demand for global goods has improved (as we anticipated in previous reports), global markets in more cyclical regions—including manufacturing-heavy Europe and Japan, as well as EM and EAFE—have made notable gains against the less cyclically sensitive U.S. market: Year-to-date, the MSCI World Ex-U.S. Index has outperformed the MSCI U.S. Index by 15%—its largest calendar-year gap since 1993.4
  • The U.S. dollar, historically a countercyclical currency, weakened as investors rotated out of expensive U.S. stocks and into more attractively valued and faster-growing non-U.S. markets.
  • Credit spreads, often sensitive to rising economic stress, have widened only modestly.5

Despite these signals, we believe there is room for further volatility, especially among U.S. high-growth stocks that, as shown in figure 1, still appear roughly 57% more expensive than their deep-value peers.6 We believe this leaves room for further derating of growth stocks relative to value stocks. Even if growth stocks were to meet their elevated earnings expectations, we worry that compressing valuations could hurt their returns relative to value. Against this backdrop, we continue favoring a tilt toward value over growth.

Figure 1: High-Growth Stocks Still Trade at Significant Premia Compared to Their Value Peers, Perhaps Allowing Room for More Short-Term Pain Ahead

Slowing Growth vs. Recession

As the trade war intensifies, we believe the chances that U.S. economic growth could slow sharply in the near term have risen. The IMF estimates that higher tariffs, including retaliation from China and the eurozone, could lower U.S. GDP by a full two percentage points over the next 12 months.7 If the U.S. administration also achieves its goal of cutting $1 trillion in government spending, that could shave another 0.8% from growth, with the greatest impact felt in the Q4, in our view. These potential hits to growth are big enough to raise recessionary concerns.

We believe handicapping the odds of a recession starts with assessing an economy’s strength at the onset of the exogenous shock—in short, the stronger the U.S. economy was before the tariffs were announced, the more resilient it might prove in absorbing their impacts. Heading into April, we believe the U.S. economy lacked the conditions—overinvestment, restricted credit availability and financially strapped consumers—that we view as often necessary to convert shocks into recessions.

Instead, we find that the cyclical parts of the economy appear to have been strengthening. Furthermore, economically sensitive sectors, which represent roughly half of all non-farm jobs in the U.S., have been adding employees at an increasing pace for the past six months8 (see the left side of figure 2).

Underpinning this cyclical strength are goods orders that are once again picking up9 after a roughly two-year slump that followed overordering by homebound consumers during the COVID-19 pandemic. Additionally, as economic growth has broadened, rising sales and profits have incentivized companies to increase capacity, buy everyday equipment to support an expanding workforce, and replace depreciated assets, further increasing the demand for goods.

Meanwhile, real disposable personal income—which makes up 75% of U.S. GDP—grew at a solid 3.0% annual rate over the past six months,10 and U.S. households had added roughly $14 trillion to their net worth in 2024.11 We believe that this wealth effect boosted discretionary purchases relative to essentials and reignited demand for goods.12

Furthermore, lending standards for both consumers and businesses had been easing, economy-wide tax receipts had accelerated to a cycle high,13 and U.S. aggregate final demand (domestic GDP net of exports and inventory changes) had clocked a solid 3.8% annualized growth as of 4Q 2024 (see the right side of figure 2). We believe these figures are consistent with an economy that could absorb significant blows to growth from tariffs and spending cuts, and avoid recession.

Assuming there are no further significant shocks, we expect U.S. GDP to grow between 0 and 1% over the next 12 months (helped by a potentially falling dollar, interest rates and energy prices). We also expect earnings growth for the S&P 500 Index to flatline over the same period, with rising inflation from tariffs only partially offsetting the growth hit.

While a recession is still not our base case, we acknowledge that a worsening trade war, further fiscal austerity, or yet another exogenous shock on an already weakened economy could ultimately trigger one. We estimate a mild recession might drag the S&P 500 Index into the high 4000s, while a moderate recession could pull it into the low to mid-4000s.

Figure 2: Market Turmoil Aside, Cyclical Employment and Demand Has Been Strengthening

The Industrial Rebound: Dented, Delayed But Not Derailed

In our 1Q 2025 Equity Market Outlook, we foresaw a gradual rebound in global industrial activity, which had been in a two-year slump. Thus far into the year, data have been unusually volatile, making it difficult to discern signs of a definitive upturn.14 However, there have been some encouraging signals: The global PMI is off its 2024 trough;15 intentions by large companies to boost corporate capex remain vastly above their long-term average;16 and global trade volume continues to accelerate (as shown on the left side of figure 3).17

Figure 3: While Tariffs May Temporarily Dampen Industrial Activity, Nascent Signs of an Industrial Recovery and a Recent Upturn in Global Trade Could Lend Support to Value and Small Cap Stocks

While we acknowledge that tariffs could dent, delay and soften the rebound in the $28 trillion global industrial economy, we do not believe they will completely derail it. The market seems to agree, which to us probably explains why goods-oriented sectors, regions and styles have been outperforming during the recent sell-off.

It is worth noting that, on average, industrial cycles exhibit acceleration and deceleration phases that each tend to last two to three years, with peak growth rates as high as 10 – 20% year-on-year.18 In our view, this historical trend implies that, without fresh tariffs, industrial production can potentially add $3 – 5 trillion of new industrial growth in peak years. Furthermore, we believe increased reshoring momentum due to tariffs could significantly accelerate the next upturn in the industrial cycle.

While tariffs are bound to impede growth, we believe underlying momentum in the goods economy will likely support the outperformance of equities that are most levered to it—including value and small-cap stocks (see the right side of figure 3), as well as non-U.S., goods-dominated markets such as Europe and Japan.

Portfolio Considerations: Regions and Sectors

Europe: Upgrading to Overweight

The policy contrast between the U.S. and Europe is stark: While the Trump administration seems set on constraining government outlays, EU policymakers appear to be in expansion mode and looking to contribute more significantly toward economic growth over the next several years.

In addition to accommodative policy (fiscal and monetary), European corporate earnings are significantly levered to the recently accelerating global goods economy, suggesting to us that Europe could post faster EPS growth than the U.S. We believe this could set the stage for the European equity market to outperform the U.S. and the world in the medium term.

The importance of faster earnings growth has been on display for a while. Consider that between 2010 and 2022, European stocks lagged as U.S. companies increased earnings per share by 14% per year, compared to just 2% per year for their European peers.19 Then the script flipped: Since the fourth quarter of 2022, European companies have increased earnings by 12% per year versus just 5% for U.S. companies, as shown in the left side of figure 4. Faster earnings growth has led the MSCI Europe Index to steadily outperform the S&P 500 Index excluding NVIDIA, which has been a primary driver of the broad index’s recent outperformance (see the right side).

Figure 4: As Corporate Earnings Have Grown Faster in Europe Than in the U.S., the MSCI Europe Index Has Handily Outperformed the S&P 500 Index (After Removing High-flying AI Chipmaker Nvidia)

Meanwhile, Europe’s near-term economic momentum also appears to be gaining strength. Economic gauges—including the Eurocoin indicator,20 the Sentix survey,21 the ZEW indicator22 and IFO Business Climate Index23—hint that growth is accelerating across the continent. In addition, growth in real M1 money supply recently turned positive,24 with such rapid improvements often followed by upturns in economic activity. The ECB has also been easing policy, with more rate cuts likely to follow. Given the typical 12- to 18-month lag between monetary easing and economic activity in Europe, we believe any additional rate cuts could boost growth well into 2026.

We also believe cyclical sectors are gaining momentum, as evidenced by substantial increases in housing values and credit demand. Residential property prices in Spain, Italy, Germany and the Netherlands have all surged,25 while mortgage, consumer credit and corporate lending activity have also risen.26 After a decade of extensive deleveraging in southern Europe, household balance sheets are in excellent condition. With debt service ratios at multi-decade lows and personal savings rates at a substantial 15%,27 we believe consumers are much more likely to take advantage of monetary easing by reducing excess savings and boosting consumption, thereby potentially driving growth more broadly across Europe.

In our view, there are even more reasons to feel optimistic about Europe's prospects over the medium term. We expect growth to benefit from higher defense spending, potential structural reforms, banking consolidation, lower oil prices and Ukraine’s post-war reconstruction—a scenario that appears to be priced into the Russian ruble, which is up 30% against the USD year-to-date.28

Finally, global investors remain substantially underweight Europe. In fact, only about 5% of the assets that flowed out of Europe at the start of the Ukraine conflict in early 2022 have returned.29 Meanwhile, the relative P/E ratio for the MSCI Europe Index compared to the S&P 500 Index remains two standard deviations below its long-term average, near a five-decade low (see figure 5).30 With plentiful room for relative revaluation, we favor an overweight stance toward European equities in a global portfolio.

Figure 5: European Stocks Remain Historically Cheap Relative to the S&P 500 Index

China: Maintaining Overweight

We believe China’s top tech firms are emerging as global contenders to the U.S. Magnificent 7 as President Xi Jinping aims to boost innovation in critical areas such as AI and robotics. Since March 2024, the combined market cap of the China 7,31 which accounts for 43% of the MSCI China Index,32 has grown by 54%, and yet it represents just one-ninth of the combined market cap of the Mag 7.33 Even though China’s leading tech companies appear overbought more recently, the gaping size differential with U.S. megacaps suggests to us that the China 7 still has plenty of room to run relative to the Mag 7. (For more on China’s growing support for its tech sector, see my colleague Yan Taw Boon’s article “Forget the Magnificent 7: Meet China’s Terrific 10”.)

As for China’s beleaguered real estate market, Tier I and Tier II cities have seen faster clearance times,34 reaccelerating transaction volumes and rising property prices35—signs that we believe might point to an early-stage rebound; we believe these trends indicate the early stage of a potential rebound that could broaden to Tier III cities, where prices are still falling.

Sagging home prices tend to stoke feelings of financial insecurity and curb spending; as the housing market has regained some footing and policymakers seek to fortify the social safety net, we believe consumers may have incentive to spend more and save less, driving retail sales growth and broadening China’s equity rally beyond the tech sector.

Meanwhile, China has been easing policy via lower lending rates, bank required-reserve-ratio cuts and increased bank-capital injections.36 Also, we believe green shoots of a new credit cycle are appearing: Property prices have begun to stabilize;37 growth in the M2 money supply has risen;38 and credit growth has begun to accelerate.39 We believe there is room for further fiscal stimulus should economic growth start trailing President Xi’s 5% target.

Finally, with investor positioning in Chinese equities still relatively low, and the potential for positive economic and earnings surprises relatively high in our view, we are maintaining our overweight stance on China.

Japan: Maintaining Market Weight

We believe Japan’s equity market is especially poised to benefit from an industrial rebound in 2025 should it pan out. As shown in figure 3, among major global regions, Japan’s earnings have historically shown the greatest leverage to trade; therefore, as global demand for goods picks up, we believe Japanese stocks have the potential to outpace other global equity markets.

Meanwhile, Japan’s economy is exhibiting classic signs of strengthening. Wage growth is accelerating,40 Japan’s labor market remains tight,41 the outlook for consumer spending is brightening,42 and an increasing number of Japanese companies plan to make capex investments.43 This improved economic performance is showing up in subsurface market returns: Japanese value stocks, which tend to be more sensitive to shifts in the cyclical economy, have been outperforming Japanese growth stocks since January 2021.44

Notably, the Bank of Japan’s policy shift away from zero and negative interest rates has breathed new life into the banking system: Bank profits have doubled over the last two years due to greater deposit and lending activity; banks remain well funded; borrowing costs are low;45 and real interest rates are still negative. We believe low rates will continue to drive loan growth, spur consumer spending and boost business investment.

However, a stronger economy may not guarantee superior stock market performance. The BOJ’s benchmark policy rate, at just 0.5%, lags these economic improvements, especially when inflationary pressure is ratcheting up and wages have been rising at 2.8% (with bigger negotiated boosts still to come).46 If the BOJ is forced to tighten faster than expected, we worry that domestic growth could slow and strengthen the yen, which could hamper the relative performance of the export-heavy Japanese stock market. While we are pondering an upgrade to overweight next quarter, for now we are standing pat. (For more detail on our constructive near-term outlook on Japan, please see my colleague Kei Okamura’s recent paper, “Japanese Equities in 2025: Goldilocks Would Approve.”)

Consumer Discretionary: Downgrading to Underweight from Overweight

We believe a potentially diminishing wealth effect, reduced real income from tariff-driven inflation, anticipated public-sector layoffs and declining consumer confidence may increase the personal savings rate, putting downward pressure on spending growth.

Financials: Downgrading to Underweight from Overweight

The financial sector tends to be strongly levered to economic growth. While we do not anticipate a significant economic downturn (see the first section of this paper), we believe tariff impacts, DOGE cuts and increased overall uncertainty may ultimately dampen growth. In our view, slower growth, a flattening yield curve and reduced business activity would likely crimp profits at banks and financial institutions, making them less attractive to investors. Until uncertainty subsides, we remain cautious on the sector.

Energy: Upgrading to Overweight From Underweight

A combination of improving fundamentals and stabilizing risks has increased our optimism on the energy sector. While the elevated risk premium in oil prices seems to be retreating in hopes of de-escalating conflict in Ukraine, we think rising global industrial activity will continue to buoy oil demand and keep prices aloft. Additionally, we believe energy stocks appear to be the most undervalued sector in the S&P 500 relative to its current and potential earnings growth. We think much of the negative news—including regulatory concerns and geopolitical risks—appears to be priced in, suggesting to us that energy stocks could rise as sentiment improves.

Risks to the U.S. Market

Despite the recent turmoil, less than 1% of the inflows into U.S. stocks since the presidential election have been reversed.47 We believe this positioning could make the market susceptible to negative news and economic surprises—and we can imagine a few.

First, if the stock market continues to stall, the wealth effect could fade, and with it the resilience of the U.S. economy to exogenous shocks. We believe that rising wealth helped boost U.S. consumption, which contributed a solid 74% of the growth in U.S. GDP over the past eight quarters.48 Yet net worth likely declined last quarter (see the left side of figure 6).

A negative wealth effect isn’t the only threat to consumption, in our view. Reduced immigration, further cutting by the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE), and reaccelerating inflation (which reduces real disposable personal income) could also curb consumer spending. Given the pickup in cyclical employment, we don’t expect job growth to turn meaningfully negative in 2025 even in the face of cuts in the public sector; however, aggregate job growth should slow down as DOGE layoffs begin to gather momentum later this year, possibly edging the unemployment rate upward.

Figure 6: Potential Risks Include a Fading Wealth Effect and a Struggling Housing Market

Rising uncertainty from flip-flopping policy maneuvers could also lead to slower growth. With core CPI running above 3%, the Federal Reserve may not be able to cut rates in time to counteract waning consumption and reduced government spending. If economic growth were to falter, long-term interest rates could fall, reinverting the yield curve. While this scenario is not our base case, we believe an inverting yield curve can offer timely clues that growth is slowing significantly faster than we expect.

Finally, we believe the U.S. housing market is clearly struggling. Affordability is historically low;49 mortgage applications remain anemic;50 finished new homes are at a cycle high;51 total units under construction have been falling;52 and construction job openings have plunged (see the right side of figure 6). Those headwinds have sent U.S. homebuilder stocks down over 25% since last October,53 and we fear further weakness in this group could portend broader economic softening ahead.

Index Definitions

The S&P 500 Index consists of 500 U.S. stocks chosen for market size, liquidity and industry group representation. It is a market value-weighted index (stock price times number of shares outstanding), with each stock’s weight in the Index proportionate to its market value.

The Russell 1000® Growth Index measures the performance of the large-cap growth segment of the U.S. equity universe. It includes those Russell 1000 companies with relatively higher price-to-book ratios, higher I/B/E/S forecast medium term (2-year) growth and higher sales per share historical growth (5 years).

The Russell 1000® Value Index measures the performance of the large-cap value segment of the U.S. equity universe. It includes those Russell 1000 companies with relatively lower price-to-book ratios, lower I/B/E/S forecast medium term (2-year) growth and lower sales per share historical growth (5 years).

The MSCI ACWI Index captures large and mid-cap representation across 23 Developed Markets (DM) and 24 Emerging Markets (EM) countries. DM countries include: Australia, Austria, Belgium, Canada, Denmark, Finland, France, Germany, Hong Kong, Ireland, Israel, Italy, Japan, Netherlands, New Zealand, Norway, Portugal, Singapore, Spain, Sweden, Switzerland, the U.K. and the U.S. EM countries include: Brazil, Chile, China, Colombia, Czech Republic, Egypt, Greece, Hungary, India, Indonesia, Korea, Kuwait, Malaysia, Mexico, Peru, Philippines, Poland, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, South Africa, Taiwan, Thailand, Turkey and United Arab Emirates. With 2,837 constituents, the index covers approximately 85% of the global investable equity opportunity set.

The MSCI China Index captures large and mid-cap representation across China A shares, H shares, B shares, Red chips, P chips and foreign listings (e.g., ADRs). With 655 constituents, the index covers about 85% of this China equity universe.

The MSCI Japan Index is designed to measure the performance of the large and mid-cap segments of the Japanese market. With 203 constituents, the index covers approximately 85% of the free float-adjusted market capitalization in Japan.

The MSCI EAFE Index is an equity index which captures large and mid-cap representation across 21 Developed Markets countries around the world, excluding the U.S. and Canada. With 741 constituents, the index covers approximately 85% of the free float-adjusted market capitalization in each country.

The MSCI EM Index captures large and mid-cap representation across Emerging Markets countries. The index covers approximately 85% of the free float-adjusted market capitalization in each country.

The MSCI Europe Index captures large and mid-cap representation across Developed Markets countries in Europe. The index covers approximately 85% of the free float-adjusted market capitalization across the European Developed Markets equity universe.

The MSCI USA Index is designed to measure the performance of the large and mid-cap segments of the U.S. market. The index covers approximately 85% of the free float-adjusted market capitalization in the US.

The S&P SmallCap 600 Index seeks to measure the small-cap segment of the U.S. equity market. The index is designed to track companies that meet specific inclusion criteria to ensure that they are liquid and financially viable.

data.title: Investor Fear Is Skyrocketing: These 5 Shocking Facts Could Help You Cash In
data.description:
There is widespread fear about this trade war due to the alarming numbers.

245% tariffs on China! 29.8% global weighted tariffs! The highest since 1909 and 50% higher than the Smoot-Hawley tariffs in the Depression!

The charts showing a decline in consumer confidence are alarming.

We've never seen such fear from consumers about essential topics like inflation, employment, or the economy itself.

12-month inflation expectations are the highest since 1981, almost 7%!

After 27% inflation since 2020, that would be a substantial financial blow...if it actually happens.

Trucking rates have fallen by 35% in the last two weeks, a decline that typically occurs only during severe recessions.

My company now has access to some truly incredible statistical risk management simulation tools, which enable us to address the most pressing questions currently on the minds of investors.

Questions like:

  • How bad will inflation get?
  • How weak will the economy get?
  • How bad might the bear market get?
  • Is the economy already in recession?
  • Are we headed for another global financial crisis (GFC) or a 2002-style 50% market crash?

In other words, in a world where it's hard even to know what global tariffs are, when headlines change by the hour, how bad might things get for Main Street? And Wall Street?

Let's examine these questions point by point and see what the best available data indicates.

  • Factoring in the best available consensus estimates and forecasts from reputable sources like Goldman Sachs, JPMorgan, Morgan Stanley, Oxford Economics, etc.
  • And applying millions of simulations and adjustments for inherent uncertainty and natural economic variability at any given time.

For years, I've told you that "all of Wall Street (and life) is a series of probability curves.

Now let me show you what that looks like.

Let me pull back the curtain on how the world's economy works and what those probability curves look like as of Thursday, April 17th.

Fact 1: What You Need To Know About Inflation

Here is the real-time inflation data, based on 30 million daily prices, which stands at 1.36%, a slight decrease from the previous month.

As you can see in the chart above, when tariffs first began increasing on February 4th, real-time inflation was 2.19% and has since decreased.

Ford (F) and LVMH have announced that prices will start rising in May, which might explain the lack of price increases so far.

What about inflation expectations for the future? We've seen how consumers are worried about nearly 7% inflation, and those expectations are likely to rise due to headlines like these.

The average American doesn't know how tariffs work, of that a 10% tariff usually only converts to a 4% price increase (because the tariff is on the port of entry and 60% of the value of imported products tends to be in the rest of the supply chain).

In other words, "245% tariffs on China" are not the same as "products on Chinese goods will increase by 245%"; for several reasons.

First, the 245% is the peak tariff on some items (such as syringes), but not on others (batteries are 167%).

29% of goods (technology) are still exempt, so the weighted China tariff is 175%.

This means the global weighted tariff rises from 22.5% to 29.8%, reverting to the 30% estimate provided by Bloomberg Economics on April 9th, known as the "Pause day".

The rule of thumb is that a 1% increase in tariffs raises prices by approximately 0.1% (since imports account for 11% of GDP) and results in 0.1% slower GDP growth.

  • Implying a final CPI inflation increase of 3% to 5.6%.
  • Truflation up to 4.4%

With a growth headwind of 3% and a 0.3% GDP headwind from adverse wealth effects.

  • A $8 trillion decline in US stocks equals approximately $110 billion in lower retail spending, according to Moody's.

That's approximately a 3.3% GDP impact, in an overly simplified model.

  • The global economy comprises 8.33 billion economic entities, making 28 trillion daily decisions, so no model is even close to being comprehensive.

OK, but what about the best long-term inflation forecaster, the bond market? That $36 trillion liquid "risk-free" market that serves as the basis of global finance?

Statistically Adjusted CPI Inflation Rates Over The Next Year

1. The consensus tariff shock (+0.8 – 1.5 ppt midpoint). 2. Major‑bank forecast dispersion (Goldman, MS, Oxford). 3. Historical CPI volatility (σ ≈ 1.2 ppt).

What is the probability of 6.7% inflation, the most recent University of Michigan 12-month consumer expectation?

  • 0.0233% or 1 in 4,299.
  • That's 47X less likely than nuclear war in any given year.

Fact 2: How Bad Could A Recession Be

Okay, so recession risk is elevated, but not a sure thing by any means. But what does that mean for how weak GDP might get?
